faqSubtitle: Everything you need to know about HookSniff
a1: HookSniff is a webhook delivery service for developers. We reliably deliver webhooks to your endpoints with automatic retries, HMAC signatures, and real-time monitoring. Think of it as a postal service for your HTTP callbacks.
q2: How is HookSniff different from Svix or Hookdeck?
a2: HookSniff offers similar features at a lower cost. Our Pro plan is $24/mo vs Svix at $490/mo. We also support 11 SDKs, FIFO delivery, CloudEvents, and a schema registry. We focus on being developer-friendly with transparent pricing.
a3: Yes. HookSniff handles webhook deliveries with a 99.9% uptime SLA. We use PostgreSQL for durable storage, automatic retries with exponential backoff, and dead letter queues for failed deliveries.
a4: Sign up for a free account at hooksniff.vercel.app, get your API key, and create an endpoint. Send your first webhook in under 5 minutes with our quick start guide or SDK.
q5: Do I need a credit card to start?
a5: No. The free plan includes 1,000 events per day with no credit card required. Upgrade to Startup, Pro, or Enterprise when you need more.
q6: What payment methods do you accept?
a6: We accept all major credit cards via Polar.sh for global payments and iyzico for Turkish customers. Plans are billed monthly or annually (20% discount on annual).
q7: Can I change my plan at any time?
a7: Yes. Upgrade or downgrade anytime from your dashboard. Upgrades take effect immediately with prorated billing. Downgrades take effect at the end of your current billing period.
q8: What happens if I exceed my plan limits?
a8: If you exceed your daily event limit, additional events may be rejected until your limit resets. We will notify you so you can upgrade if needed. Retries and failed deliveries do not count against your limit.
a9: Failed deliveries are retried with exponential backoff: 30s, 60s, 120s, 5min, 10min, 30min. After max attempts (default 5), the delivery moves to a dead letter queue for debugging.
a10: Every webhook delivery includes an X-HookSniff-Signature header containing an HMAC-SHA256 hash of the payload. Verify this signature on your end to ensure the webhook came from HookSniff and wasn't tampered with.
a11: Yes. In addition to HTTP POST delivery, HookSniff supports WebSocket connections for real-time event streaming. Configure WebSocket delivery per endpoint.
q12: What is the dead letter queue?
a12: The dead letter queue stores failed deliveries that exhausted all retry attempts. You can inspect payloads, error messages, and response codes to debug issues before replaying the delivery.
a13: Yes. All data is encrypted in transit (TLS) and at rest. We use HMAC-SHA256 for webhook signatures, Argon2id for password hashing, and support 2FA via TOTP. We're SOC 2 ready.
a14: Yes, for the duration of your plan's retention period (7-365 days depending on plan). Payloads are stored encrypted and automatically deleted after the retention period. You can also delete them manually.
q15: Can I use a custom domain?
a15: Yes. Business plan customers can configure custom domains for their webhook endpoints. SSL certificates are automatically provisioned via Let's Encrypt.
cantFindAnswer: Can't find the answer you're looking for? Our support team is here to help.

pricingFaq.a: A webhook is an event delivered to your endpoint. Failed deliveries that are retried do not count as additional webhooks — retries are free. Filtered messages are also free.

pricingFaq.q: Do I need a credit card to start?
pricingFaq.a: No. You can sign up and start sending webhooks on the Free plan without a credit card. Upgrade when you need more volume or features.

pricingFaq.q: What happens if I exceed my plan limit?
pricingFaq.a: We never drop webhooks. If you exceed your monthly limit, additional events are metered at the per-plan rate. You can also set a hard cap in your dashboard settings.
Yes. A webhook includes all its retries. If a delivery fails and we retry it 3 times, that still counts as 1 webhook. We absorb the retry cost because reliable delivery is our core promise.
Yes. Upgrades take effect immediately and are prorated. Downgrades take effect at the start of your next billing cycle. No penalties, no hidden fees.
Yes! If you're an early-stage startup (pre-Series A), contact us via the contact form with your details. We offer significant discounts and extended free tiers.

pricingFaq.q: What's the difference between Pro and Business?
pricingFaq.a: Pro is for growing teams that need more volume and better monitoring. Business is for teams that need SLA guarantees, FIFO delivery, SSO, and dedicated support.
Yes. Annual billing saves you 20% compared to monthly. That's $278/year for Pro and $950/year for Business.

pricingFaq.q: What payment methods do you accept?
pricingFaq.a: We accept all major credit cards via Stripe. For Business and Enterprise plans, we also support invoicing and bank transfers.
Yes! HookSniff is open-source. You can self-host with Docker. Self-hosted instances get community support. Paid plans add priority support for self-hosted.
We support 11 SDKs: Node.js, Python, Rust, C#, Go, Swift, PHP, Elixir, Java, Kotlin, and Ruby.
Yes. All data is encrypted in transit (TLS 1.3) and at rest. We use HMAC-SHA256 for webhook signatures, Argon2 for password hashing, and support 2FA/TOTP.
Yes. We are GDPR compliant. We process data in EU regions, support data export/deletion requests, and have a published privacy policy.
Free and Pro plans have a 99.9% uptime target (best effort). Business plans include a 99.9% uptime SLA with credits if we miss it.

pricingFaq.q: How does HookSniff compare to Svix?
pricingFaq.a: HookSniff offers similar features at 10x lower cost. Svix starts at $490/month; HookSniff Pro is $49/month. We also support more SDKs (11 vs 6), FIFO delivery, and CloudEvents.
Yes. You can export all your webhook logs, endpoint configurations, and delivery history as JSON or CSV from the dashboard.

about.title: About HookSniff
about.cta: Join developers who trust HookSniff for their webhook delivery.
about.heroSubtitle: Reliable webhook delivery infrastructure built by developers, for developers.
about.missionP1: Webhooks are the backbone of modern integrations, but building reliable webhook infrastructure is hard. We built HookSniff to make it simple — send webhooks, we deliver them. If they fail, we retry.
about.missionP2: We believe developers shouldn't have to pay enterprise prices for reliable webhook delivery. That's why HookSniff starts at $0/month with a generous free tier.
about.storyP1: HookSniff started as a side project in 2026. We were tired of dealing with unreliable webhook deliveries and expensive third-party services. So we built our own.
about.storyP2: Today, HookSniff handles webhook delivery for developers worldwide, with support for HTTP, WebSocket, gRPC, and SQS delivery methods. Our infrastructure runs entirely on free-tier services, keeping costs at $0/month while maintaining enterprise-grade reliability.
about.storyP3: We're committed to keeping HookSniff affordable and accessible. As we grow, we'll continue to offer a generous free tier and transparent pricing.
about.securityFirstDesc: HMAC-SHA256 signatures, TLS encryption, and zero-knowledge payload processing.
about.transparentPricingDesc: No hidden fees, no surprise charges. What you see is what you pay.
about.globalInfrastructureDesc: Deployed in EU (Frankfurt) with CDN coverage worldwide via Cloudflare.

compare.faq1q: Is HookSniff really cheaper than Svix?
compare.faq1a: Yes. HookSniff Pro is $24/mo vs Svix Professional at $490/mo. That's $5,592/year saved. HookSniff even offers a free tier with 10,000 events/month.
compare.faq2q: Why is HookSniff SOC 2 "Ready" but not "Type 2"?
compare.faq2a: SOC 2 Type 2 requires a 3–12 month audit period. HookSniff has implemented all required controls and is undergoing the audit. We expect Type 2 certification soon.
compare.faq3a: Yes. HookSniff is open-source (MIT license) and supports Docker deployment. You can run the entire stack on your own infrastructure.

compare.faq5q: What is FIFO delivery and why does it matter?
compare.faq5a: FIFO (First In, First Out) guarantees events are delivered in order. Critical for sequential operations like order status updates, payment flows, and state machines. Only HookSniff offers this.
compare.faq6a: Yes. 1,378 tests, OpenTelemetry observability, SSRF protection, HMAC signing, GDPR compliance, and it runs on Google Cloud Run with Neon PostgreSQL.
compare.faq7q: Can I migrate from Svix to HookSniff?
compare.faq7a: Yes. HookSniff uses the Standard Webhooks spec (same as Svix), so migration is straightforward. Your existing HMAC verification code will work as-is.
compare.faq8a: Not yet. HIPAA compliance requires a BAA and external audit. It's on our roadmap. Svix currently supports HIPAA.

whatIsWebhook.pizzaWebhookDesc: = The pizza place calls YOU when your pizza is ready.

whatIsWebhook.step2Desc: A payment succeeds, a user signs up, an order ships.
whatIsWebhook.step3: The service sends a POST request
whatIsWebhook.step3Desc: It sends the event data (payload) to your URL.

whatIsWebhook.ucAiDesc: An AI agent sends a webhook when a task completes.
whatIsWebhook.ucMonitoringDesc: An alert system sends a webhook when a server goes down.
whatIsWebhook.securityDesc: Webhooks are sent over HTTP, so anyone can send a request to your URL. You need to verify that the request actually came from the expected service.

whatIsWebhook.gsStep4: Process the event data and take action
whatIsWebhook.gsStep5: Return a 200 status code to acknowledge receipt

buildVsBuy.title: Build vs Buy
buildVsBuy.whenBuilding: When Does Building In-House Make Sense?
buildVsBuy.timeToMarketHooksniff: Days — deploy on free-tier infrastructure in under an hour
buildVsBuy.timeToMarketWhy: Every month you spend building webhook infrastructure is a month your competitors are shipping features. Fast time-to-market means faster revenue, faster feedback loops, and faster iteration.
buildVsBuy.engineeringCostHooksniff: $24/mo (Pro plan) — no engineers needed for maintenance
buildVsBuy.engineeringCostBuild: 3–5 engineers × 6–12 months = $300K–$1M+ initial build, then 1–2 engineers on-call permanently
buildVsBuy.engineeringCostWhy: Webhook infrastructure looks simple (just HTTP POST, right?) but hides distributed-systems complexity: durable queues, retry logic, dead-letter handling, SSRF protection, signing, replay protection, FIFO ordering, log retention, and a self-serve portal. Each is a multi-week project.
buildVsBuy.maintenanceBuild: On-call rotations, customer bug reports, infrastructure scaling, security patches, compliance audits
buildVsBuy.maintenanceWhy: Webhooks are infrastructure your customers rely on daily. When they break, it's visible: missed events, out-of-order deliveries, 5xx errors. Your best engineers get pulled off core product to debug webhook issues.
buildVsBuy.reliabilityBuild: Whatever you build — typically 99% at best, with no external accountability
buildVsBuy.reliabilityWhy: Webhook failures are customer-facing. When your webhook system goes down, your customers' integrations break. They notice. They complain. They churn.
buildVsBuy.securityHooksniff: HMAC-SHA256, SSRF protection, constant-time comparison, Argon2id, 2FA, GDPR — all built-in
buildVsBuy.securityBuild: You need to implement: HMAC signing, SSRF blocking (private IPs, metadata endpoints, DNS rebinding), replay protection, rate limiting, input validation, secret rotation, audit logging
buildVsBuy.securityWhy: Webhook-specific security is a niche expertise. SSRF attacks through webhook URLs are a real threat. One misconfiguration can expose internal infrastructure.
buildVsBuy.dxHooksniff: 11 SDKs, webhook playground, schema registry, CloudEvents, 8-language dashboard, embeddable portal
buildVsBuy.dxBuild: Build your own SDKs, playground, portal, documentation — or leave developers to figure it out themselves
buildVsBuy.dxWhy: Your webhook consumers are developers. A poor DX means more support tickets, slower adoption, and frustrated users who switch to competitors with better webhook experiences.
buildVsBuy.scalabilityHooksniff: Auto-scales on GCP Cloud Run — handles millions of events
buildVsBuy.scalabilityBuild: You build it: connection pooling, queue management, backpressure handling, noisy-neighbor isolation
buildVsBuy.scalabilityWhy: Webhook traffic is spiky. Black Friday, product launches, viral moments — your system needs to handle 10x normal load without dropping events.
buildVsBuy.complianceHooksniff: SOC 2 ready, GDPR compliant (EU hosting), CCPA, Standard Webhooks spec
buildVsBuy.complianceBuild: SOC 2 audit alone takes 3–6 months and $50K+. GDPR compliance requires data residency controls, export, deletion endpoints
buildVsBuy.complianceWhy: Enterprise customers won't integrate without SOC 2. EU customers need GDPR compliance. Building compliance from scratch is a 6-month detour.
buildVsBuy.retryHooksniff: Exponential backoff with jitter, dead letter queue, configurable retry policies, FIFO ordering
buildVsBuy.retryBuild: Implement: exponential backoff, jitter, dead-letter queues, message deduplication, sequence numbers, idempotency keys
buildVsBuy.retryWhy: Retries sound simple until you realize you need: exponential backoff with jitter (to avoid thundering herd), dead-letter queues (for permanently failed events), deduplication (to avoid double-processing), and FIFO ordering (for sequential events).
buildVsBuy.observabilityHooksniff: OpenTelemetry (314 instrumentation points), structured JSON logging, Grafana Cloud integration
buildVsBuy.observabilityBuild: Build your own: distributed tracing, metrics collection, log aggregation, alerting dashboards
buildVsBuy.observabilityWhy: When a customer says 'I didn't receive my webhook,' you need to trace: was it sent? Did it fail? Why? How many retries? What was the response? Without observability, you're debugging blind.
buildVsBuy.portalHooksniff: Embeddable portal — your customers manage their own webhook subscriptions, view logs, replay events
buildVsBuy.portalBuild: Build a full CRUD UI: endpoint management, log viewer, replay functionality, secret rotation, event filtering
buildVsBuy.portalWhy: Without a self-serve portal, every webhook issue becomes a support ticket. 'Can you check if my webhook was delivered?' 'Can you replay these 50 events?' 'Can you add a new endpoint?' — all manual work.
buildVsBuy.multiTenancyHooksniff: Built-in — isolate webhook traffic per customer, per endpoint, with per-tenant rate limiting
buildVsBuy.multiTenancyBuild: Architect tenant isolation from scratch: data separation, rate limiting per tenant, billing per tenant, API key management
buildVsBuy.multiTenancyWhy: One noisy customer shouldn't affect others. Multi-tenancy requires careful isolation at every layer: storage, processing, rate limiting, and monitoring.
buildVsBuy.whenBuildItem1: Hobbyist and research workloads where reliability isn't critical
buildVsBuy.whenBuildItem2: Very low volume (fewer than 1,000 events/day) with no SLA requirements
buildVsBuy.whenBuildItem3: Unusual data-residency or air-gapped deployments where cloud services can't be used
buildVsBuy.whenBuildItem4: You already have a mature event infrastructure team with deep distributed-systems expertise
buildVsBuy.whenBuildNote: Even in these cases, consider the
buildVsBuy.whenBuildNote2: HookSniff open-source server (MIT license) as a starting point.
buildVsBuy.faq1q: When does building in-house make sense?
buildVsBuy.faq1a: For hobbyist projects, very low volume where reliability doesn't matter, or unusual data-residency/air-gapped deployments. Even then, consider using the HookSniff open-source server (MIT license) as a starting point.
buildVsBuy.faq2q: What about using an open-source webhook library instead?
buildVsBuy.faq2a: Libraries handle signing and maybe retries, but not: durable queues, dead-letter handling, multi-tenancy, a self-serve portal, observability, SSRF protection, rate limiting, or compliance. You still build 80% of the system.
buildVsBuy.faq3q: Can't I just use a message queue (RabbitMQ, SQS) for webhooks?
buildVsBuy.faq3a: Message queues solve delivery but not: HTTP-specific concerns (retries with backoff, status codes, timeouts), signing/verification, a consumer portal, SSRF protection, or compliance. You'll build a webhook system on top of the queue anyway.
buildVsBuy.faq4q: How much does it really cost to build webhook infrastructure?
buildVsBuy.faq4a: Industry data: 3–5 engineers × 6–12 months = $300K–$1M+ initial. Then 1–2 engineers on-call ($200K–$400K/year ongoing). Plus infrastructure, compliance audits, and opportunity cost of not building your core product.
buildVsBuy.faq5q: What if HookSniff gets acquired or shuts down?
buildVsBuy.faq5a: HookSniff is open-source (MIT license). You can self-host the entire system, fork the code, or migrate to another provider. You're never locked in.
buildVsBuy.faq6a: Yes. 1,378 tests, OpenTelemetry observability, SSRF protection, HMAC signing, GDPR compliance, and it runs on Google Cloud Run with Neon PostgreSQL. 11 SDKs published to npm, PyPI, crates.io, NuGet, Maven Central, and more.
buildVsBuy.ctaTitle: Ready to stop building and start shipping?
buildVsBuy.ctaSubtitle: Deploy HookSniff in under an hour. Free tier available. No credit card required.

alternatives.whenToChooseConvoyDesc: Convoy is a good fit for Go-native teams that want a lightweight, self-hosted webhook service. If your entire stack is Go and you prefer minimal dependencies, Convoy's single-language focus can be an advantage.
alternatives.whenToChooseWebhookRelayDesc: Webhook Relay is useful if you need webhook forwarding/tunneling for local development or simple relay use cases. It serves a different niche than full webhook delivery platforms.

whatIsHookSniff.title: What is HookSniff?
whatIsHookSniff.subtitle: HookSniff is a webhook delivery platform. You send events via API, HookSniff delivers them to your endpoints with retries, signatures, and full observability.
whatIsHookSniff.theProblemDesc: When your application needs to send webhooks, you end up building:
whatIsHookSniff.problemRetryDesc: What happens when the receiving server is down?
whatIsHookSniff.problemQueueDesc: How do you handle thousands of concurrent deliveries?
whatIsHookSniff.problemSignatureDesc: How does the receiver know the webhook is really from you?
whatIsHookSniff.problemMonitoringDesc: How do you debug failed deliveries?
whatIsHookSniff.problemRateLimitDesc: How do you protect your customers' servers from being overwhelmed?
whatIsHookSniff.problemConclusion: Building this yourself takes weeks. HookSniff handles all of it out of the box.
whatIsHookSniff.without1: Webhook code scattered across your application
whatIsHookSniff.without2: No centralized visibility on delivery status
whatIsHookSniff.without3: Retry and failure logic you maintain yourself
whatIsHookSniff.without4: Hard to debug when webhooks fail
whatIsHookSniff.without5: No signature standard — each integration is different
whatIsHookSniff.with1: One API call to send any webhook
whatIsHookSniff.with2: Dashboard shows every delivery with full details
whatIsHookSniff.with4: Dead Letter Queue for failed deliveries
whatIsHookSniff.step1: Your application sends an event to HookSniff via REST API
whatIsHookSniff.step2: HookSniff validates the payload, checks rate limits, and stores the delivery
whatIsHookSniff.step3: The worker picks up the delivery and sends an HTTP POST to your endpoint
whatIsHookSniff.step4: The payload is signed with HMAC-SHA256 using your endpoint's signing secret
whatIsHookSniff.step5: If the delivery fails, it's automatically retried (default: 3 attempts with exponential backoff)
whatIsHookSniff.step6: After all retries are exhausted, the delivery moves to the Dead Letter Queue
whatIsHookSniff.step7: Every attempt is logged with status code, response body, and timing — visible in the dashboard
whatIsHookSniff.conceptEndpointDesc: A URL where webhooks are delivered. Each endpoint has a signing secret and optional event filters.
whatIsHookSniff.conceptWebhookDesc: An event you send via the API. HookSniff delivers it to the target endpoint and tracks every attempt.
whatIsHookSniff.conceptEventTypeDesc: A string identifier like order.created that describes what happened. Used for filtering and routing.
whatIsHookSniff.conceptRetryPolicyDesc: Configurable per endpoint. Default: 3 attempts with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s). After exhaustion, delivery goes to DLQ.
whatIsHookSniff.conceptDlqDesc: Where failed deliveries go after all retries are exhausted. You can inspect them and replay with one click.
whatIsHookSniff.why1: Rust-powered — API and worker built with Axum/Tokio for high performance
whatIsHookSniff.why2: 11 SDKs — Node.js, Python, Go, Rust, Ruby, Java, Kotlin, PHP, C#, Elixir, Swift
whatIsHookSniff.why3: Standard Webhooks — HMAC-SHA256 signatures, industry-standard format
whatIsHookSniff.why4: Smart routing — Round-robin, latency-based, failover with fallback URLs
whatIsHookSniff.why5: FIFO delivery — Ordered delivery with sequence numbers per endpoint
whatIsHookSniff.why6: Per-endpoint throttling — Token bucket / sliding window to protect customer servers
whatIsHookSniff.why7: Inbound proxy — Receive webhooks from Stripe, GitHub, Shopify through HookSniff
whatIsHookSniff.why8: Embeddable portal — Let your customers manage their own webhook endpoints
whatIsHookSniff.why9: Free tier friendly — Runs entirely on free services ($0/month)
whatIsHookSniff.ucSaasDesc: Let customers receive webhook notifications from your product
whatIsHookSniff.ucEcommerceDesc: Send order updates, payment confirmations, shipping notifications
whatIsHookSniff.ucCiCdDesc: Notify teams about build results, deployments, failures
whatIsHookSniff.ucMicroservicesDesc: Decouple services with async event delivery
whatIsHookSniff.ucIntegrationsDesc: Connect to Slack, Discord, PagerDuty, or any HTTP endpoint
whatIsHookSniff.ucAuditDesc: Track all events and their delivery status for compliance
whatIsHookSniff.nextQuickstartDesc: Send your first webhook in 5 minutes.
whatIsHookSniff.nextArchitectureDesc: How HookSniff works under the hood.

docsSecurity.title: Webhook Security
docsSecurity.subtitle: Webhooks carry sensitive data. Without verification, anyone can send fake events to your endpoint.
docsSecurity.theProblemDesc: Webhooks are just HTTP POST requests. If your endpoint accepts any POST request without verification, an attacker can:
docsSecurity.problem1: Send fake "payment succeeded" events to give themselves free products
docsSecurity.problem2: Replay old events to trigger duplicate processing
docsSecurity.problem3: Send malformed payloads to crash your application
docsSecurity.howSecuresDesc1: Every webhook is signed using Standard Webhooks HMAC-SHA256. The signature is included in the X-HookSniff-Signature header. The receiver verifies the signature using the shared secret — if it doesn't match, the request is rejected.
docsSecurity.timestampHeader: Unix timestamp to prevent replay attacks
docsSecurity.timestampValidationDesc: Always validate the X-HookSniff-Timestamp header to prevent replay attacks. Reject webhooks older than 5 minutes:
docsSecurity.ipWhitelistingDesc: For additional security, restrict incoming webhooks to HookSniff's IP addresses:
docsSecurity.ipWhitelistingNote: Use these IPs in your firewall or reverse proxy. Note: IPs may change — fetch periodically.
docsSecurity.ssrfProtectionDesc: HookSniff blocks webhook delivery to internal/private networks to prevent SSRF attacks:
docsSecurity.check1: Always verify HMAC signatures before processing
docsSecurity.check5: Store signing secrets securely (environment variables, not code)
docsSecurity.check6: Rotate secrets periodically via the API
docsSecurity.check7: Whitelist HookSniff's outbound IPs if your firewall supports it

docsTroubleshooting.title: Sorun Giderme
docsTroubleshooting.subtitle: Bir şey çalışmıyor mu? İşte en yaygın sorunlar ve bunları nasıl düzelteceğiniz.
docsTroubleshooting.notArrivingSymptoms: Belirtiler: API üzerinden bir webhook gönderirsiniz, ancak uç noktanız asla almaz.
docsTroubleshooting.notArrivingCause1: Uç nokta pasif — Dashboard'da uç noktanın etkin olup olmadığını kontrol edin
docsTroubleshooting.notArrivingCause2: Olay filtresi uyumsuzluğu — Webhook'un olay türü, uç noktanızın filtresiyle eşleşmiyor
docsTroubleshooting.notArrivingCause3: SSRF koruması — HookSniff, localhost, özel IP'ler ve dahili alan adlarına teslimatı engeller
docsTroubleshooting.notArrivingCause4: Güvenlik duvarı engellemesi — Sunucunuz HookSniff'in giden IP'lerini engelliyor olabilir
docsTroubleshooting.notArrivingFix: Çözüm: GET /v1/webhooks ile teslimat durumunu kontrol edin ve hata mesajları için teslimat detaylarına bakın.
docsTroubleshooting.unauthorizedCause1: API anahtarı eksik veya hatalı — Bearer hr_live_... formatında olmalı
docsTroubleshooting.unauthorizedCause3: Programmatik erişim için API anahtarı yerine JWT token kullanılıyor
docsTroubleshooting.unauthorizedFix: Çözüm: Dashboard'da yeni bir API anahtarı oluşturun ve ortam değişkeninizi güncelleyin.
docsTroubleshooting.signatureSymptoms: Belirtiler: Uç noktanız webhook'ları "Geçersiz imza" ile reddediyor.
docsTroubleshooting.signatureCause1: Yanlış gizli anahtar — Uç noktanın mevcut imza gizli anahtarını kullanın, eskisini değil
docsTroubleshooting.signatureCause2: Gövde değişikliği — Express.js body parser ham gövdeyi değiştirir. express.raw() kullanın
docsTroubleshooting.signatureCause3: Kodlama uyumsuzluğu — JSON.stringify(parsed) yerine ham istek gövdesine karşı doğrulayın
docsTroubleshooting.signatureCause4: Gizli anahtar rotasyonu — Eski gizli anahtarlar rotasyondan sonra 24 saat boyunca geçerli kalır
docsTroubleshooting.signatureFix: Çözüm: Doğrulama kodu için Güvenlik sayfasına bakın.
docsTroubleshooting.rateLimitedSymptoms: Belirtiler: API istekleri 429 Çok Fazla İstek döndürüyor.
docsTroubleshooting.rateLimitedCause: Neden: Planınızın dakika başına istek limitini aştınız.
docsTroubleshooting.rateLimitedFix: Çözüm: Retry-After başlık süresini bekleyin, ardından üstel geri çekilme ile tekrar deneyin. Detaylar için Hız Sınırlama sayfasına bakın.
docsTroubleshooting.stuckSymptoms: Belirtiler: Webhook'lar uzun süre "beklemede" olarak görünüyor.
docsTroubleshooting.stuckCause1: Worker çalışmıyor — Teslimat worker'ı çalışmıyor olabilir
docsTroubleshooting.stuckCause2: Kuyruk birikmesi — Yüksek hacim gecikmelere neden olabilir
docsTroubleshooting.stuckCause3: Uç nokta zaman aşımı — Uç noktanız yanıt vermek için çok uzun sürüyor (30s limit)
docsTroubleshooting.stuckFix: Çözüm: Worker durumunu kontrol edin (kendi kendine barındırma için make status) veya dashboard sağlık göstergesini kontrol edin (bulut).
docsTroubleshooting.highFailureCause1: Uç nokta 5xx döndürüyor — Sunucunuzda bir sorun var
docsTroubleshooting.highFailureCause2: Uç nokta 4xx döndürüyor — Uç noktanız istekleri reddediyor (kimlik doğrulamayı kontrol edin)
docsTroubleshooting.highFailureCause3: DNS hatası — Uç nokta URL'nize erişilemiyor
docsTroubleshooting.highFailureCause4: TLS hatası — Uç noktanızda sertifika sorunu
docsTroubleshooting.highFailureFix: Çözüm: Spesifik hata mesajları için dashboard'daki teslimat denemelerini kontrol edin.
docsTroubleshooting.limitSymptoms: Belirtiler: API, "webhook limiti aşıldı" ile 403 döndürüyor.
docsTroubleshooting.limitCause: Neden: Planınızın aylık webhook limitine ulaştınız.
docsTroubleshooting.limitFix: Çözüm: GET /v1/billing/usage ile kullanımı kontrol edin. Planınızı yükseltin veya bir sonraki fatura dönemini bekleyin.
docsTroubleshooting.helpDebugGuide: Başarısız Webhook'ları Debug Etme rehberini inceleyin

docsBestPractices.title: Webhook Best Practices
docsBestPractices.subtitle: Production patterns for both sides of a webhook integration: the producer (sender) and the consumer (receiver).
docsBestPractices.signPayloadDesc: Every outgoing webhook should include an HMAC-SHA256 signature in the headers. This lets consumers verify the payload hasn't been tampered with. HookSniff does this automatically — every delivery is signed with the endpoint's signing secret.
docsBestPractices.neverResign: Never sign a re-serialized version of the payload. Byte differences will break verification. Use the raw request body.
docsBestPractices.idempotencyKeyDesc: Add a unique identifier to each event so consumers can deduplicate:
docsBestPractices.idempotencyKeyTip: Use business logic identifiers (e.g., order-{id}-created), not random UUIDs. This ensures the same operation always produces the same key.
docsBestPractices.payloadStabilityDesc: Keep payloads self-contained. Include all the data the consumer needs rather than forcing them to make API calls back to you.
docsBestPractices.eventNamingDesc: Use resource.action format with dot separators:
docsBestPractices.eventNamingTip: Be specific: invoice.payment_failed is better than invoice.error.
docsBestPractices.verifyFirstDesc: Before processing any webhook, verify the HMAC signature. Reject requests with missing or invalid signatures immediately.
docsBestPractices.verifyFirstTip: Use constant-time comparison (hmac.compare_digest) to prevent timing attacks.
docsBestPractices.respondFastDesc: Return a 200 OK within 5 seconds. Do the actual processing asynchronously:
docsBestPractices.respondFastTip: If your endpoint takes too long, HookSniff may time out and retry, leading to duplicate processing.
docsBestPractices.handleDuplicatesDesc: Webhooks can be delivered more than once. Track processed delivery IDs:
docsBestPractices.httpsOnlyDesc: Always expose webhook endpoints over HTTPS. HookSniff only delivers to HTTPS endpoints by default. HTTP endpoints are rejected unless explicitly allowed.
docsBestPractices.monitoringDesc: Track these metrics to catch issues early:
docsBestPractices.monitoringTip: Use HookSniff's built-in alerts to get notified when these thresholds are crossed.
docsBestPractices.consistentEnvelopeDesc: Every event should follow the same top-level structure:
docsBestPractices.timestampFormatDesc: Always use ISO 8601 with timezone: 2026-01-15T10:30:00Z. Never use Unix timestamps in payloads — they are harder to read when debugging.
docsBestPractices.versionEventsDesc: When you change payload shapes, include a version in the event type or payload. Support old versions for at least 6 months after announcing deprecation. Example: order.created.v2.

docsConcepts.title: Core Concepts
docsConcepts.subtitle: Before diving into the API, understand the building blocks of HookSniff. These concepts are the foundation for everything else.
docsConcepts.whyMatterDesc: Webhook systems look simple on the surface — just send an HTTP POST. But in production, you quickly run into questions:
docsConcepts.whyQ1: What happens when the receiver's server is down?
docsConcepts.whyQ2: How do you prove the webhook really came from you?
docsConcepts.whyQ3: How do you handle thousands of webhooks without overwhelming the receiver?
docsConcepts.whyQ4: How do you debug when something goes wrong?
docsConcepts.whyConclusion: HookSniff's core concepts solve each of these problems. Here's how they fit together.
docsConcepts.endpointDesc: An endpoint is a URL where HookSniff delivers webhooks. Think of it as a mailing address for your webhooks.
docsConcepts.endpointWhy: Why endpoints matter: Instead of hardcoding URLs in your application, you register endpoints in HookSniff. This lets you change the destination without code changes, add event filters, and track delivery health per destination.
docsConcepts.endpointUrl: URL — The target that receives webhook POST requests
docsConcepts.endpointSecret: Signing secret — HMAC-SHA256 secret for payload verification (whsec_ prefix)
docsConcepts.endpointFilter: Event filter — Optional: only deliver specific event types (e.g., order.*)
docsConcepts.endpointStatus: Status — Active or inactive; inactive endpoints skip delivery
docsConcepts.endpointRetry: Retry policy — Custom retry behavior per endpoint
docsConcepts.webhookDesc: A webhook is an event you send via the API. A delivery is HookSniff's attempt to deliver that webhook to an endpoint.
docsConcepts.webhookDistinction: The distinction matters: One webhook can have multiple deliveries (retries, multiple endpoints). Each delivery is tracked independently with its own status, attempts, and timing.
docsConcepts.webhookEventTypes: Event types — String identifiers like order.created, user.updated
docsConcepts.webhookPayloads: Payloads — JSON data sent as the request body
docsConcepts.webhookStatus: Delivery status — pending, delivered, or failed
docsConcepts.webhookAttempts: Attempt tracking — Each delivery attempt is recorded with status code, response body, and duration
docsConcepts.webhookIdempotency: Idempotency — Use the Idempotency-Key header to prevent duplicate deliveries
docsConcepts.retryProblem: The problem: Servers go down, networks hiccup, deployments happen. If a webhook delivery fails, you don't want to lose the event.
docsConcepts.retrySolution: The solution: HookSniff automatically retries failed deliveries using exponential backoff with jitter. Each retry waits longer than the last, preventing thundering herd problems.
docsConcepts.retryDefault: Default schedule (3 attempts, configurable per endpoint):
docsConcepts.retryAfter: After all retries are exhausted, the delivery moves to the Dead Letter Queue.
docsConcepts.dlqProblem: The problem: After all retries fail, what happens to the event? Without a DLQ, it's silently lost.
docsConcepts.dlqSolution: The solution: Failed deliveries are preserved in the DLQ with full context — the original payload, every attempt's status code, response body, and timing. You can inspect them, understand what went wrong, and replay with one click.
docsConcepts.dlqAttempts: All attempt details (status codes, errors, timestamps)
docsConcepts.dlqReplay: Ability to replay — re-queue the delivery for another attempt
docsConcepts.dlqRetention: Retention based on plan (Developer: 7 days, Startup: 14 days, Pro: 180 days, Enterprise: 365 days)
docsConcepts.apiKeyProblem: The problem: How do you authenticate API requests without sharing your password?
docsConcepts.apiKeySolution: The solution: API keys are long-lived tokens prefixed with hr_live_. You can create multiple keys (one per service or team member) and rotate them without downtime.
docsConcepts.apiKeyHashed: Keys are Argon2 hashed in the database — plaintext is never stored
docsConcepts.apiKeyMultiple: Multiple keys per account — rotate without downtime
docsConcepts.apiKeyScoped: Scoped to your plan's rate limits
docsConcepts.apiKeyPrefix: Each key shows only its prefix in the dashboard — full key shown once at creation
docsConcepts.fifoProblem: The problem: If you send webhooks for order.created and order.updated in quick succession, the updated event might arrive before the created event. Your consumer processes them out of order.
docsConcepts.fifoSolution: The solution: HookSniff delivers webhooks in FIFO (first-in, first-out) order per endpoint. Each delivery includes a sequence number so consumers can detect gaps or reorder events.
docsConcepts.flowStep1: You create an endpoint with a URL and signing secret
docsConcepts.flowStep2: You send a webhook via API with an event type and payload
docsConcepts.flowStep3: HookSniff creates a delivery and signs the payload with HMAC-SHA256
docsConcepts.flowStep4: The worker delivers it to your endpoint in FIFO order
docsConcepts.flowStep5: If it fails, retries kick in with exponential backoff
docsConcepts.flowStep6: If all retries fail, the delivery goes to the DLQ for inspection
docsConcepts.flowStep7: You can replay failed deliveries after fixing the issue
docsConcepts.flowConclusion: All of this is visible in the dashboard with real-time delivery tracking, analytics, and alerting.

docsErrorCodes.title: Error Codes
docsErrorCodes.subtitle: Reference for all error codes returned by the HookSniff API.
docsErrorCodes.errorFormatDesc: All errors follow a consistent JSON structure:
docsErrorCodes.codeField: The code field is a machine-readable identifier. The message field is a human-readable description.
docsErrorCodes.badRequestCause: Invalid JSON, missing required fields, or validation failure
docsErrorCodes.unauthorizedCause: Missing or invalid API key, expired JWT token
docsErrorCodes.forbiddenCause: Insufficient permissions, plan limit exceeded, or admin-only endpoint
docsErrorCodes.notFoundCause: Resource doesn't exist or doesn't belong to your account
docsErrorCodes.rateLimitedCause: Too many requests — check Retry-After header
docsErrorCodes.internalErrorCause: Unexpected error — contact support if persistent
docsErrorCodes.unauthorizedDesc: Your API key is missing, invalid, or expired.
docsErrorCodes.unauthorizedFix: Check that your Authorization header is Bearer hr_live_... and the key is active in your dashboard settings.
docsErrorCodes.badRequestEndpointDesc: The endpoint ID doesn't exist or doesn't belong to your account.
docsErrorCodes.badRequestEndpointFix: List your endpoints with GET /v1/endpoints and use a valid ID.
docsErrorCodes.rateLimitedDesc: You've exceeded your plan's requests-per-minute limit.
docsErrorCodes.rateLimitedFix: Wait for the Retry-After header duration, then retry with exponential backoff. Consider upgrading your plan if this happens frequently.
docsErrorCodes.forbiddenLimitDesc: You've reached your plan's monthly webhook limit.
docsErrorCodes.forbiddenLimitFix: Check usage with GET /v1/billing/usage. Upgrade your plan or wait for the next billing period.
docsErrorCodes.payloadTooLargeDesc: The webhook payload exceeds the 1 MB limit.
docsErrorCodes.payloadTooLargeFix: Reduce payload size. Send only essential data. If you need to send large payloads, consider uploading to storage and including a URL in the webhook.
docsErrorCodes.idempotencyCached: If the same key is used within 24 hours, the cached response is returned (not an error)
docsErrorCodes.idempotencyConflict: If the same key is used with different parameters, a 409 CONFLICT is returned
docsErrorCodes.idempotencyExpire: Keys expire after 24 hours and can be safely reused

docsConfiguration.title: Configuration Reference
docsConfiguration.subtitle: All environment variables and configuration options for self-hosting HookSniff.
docsConfiguration.redisUrl: Redis connection string (optional, for rate limiting)
docsConfiguration.rustLog: Log level: trace, debug, info, warn, error
docsConfiguration.jwtSecret: Secret for JWT token signing (required, 64+ chars)
docsConfiguration.hmacSecret: Global HMAC secret for webhook signing (required)
docsConfiguration.masterApiKey: Admin API key for platform management (optional)

docsRetries.title: Retries & Retry Policy
docsRetries.subtitle: Failed deliveries shouldn't be lost. HookSniff retries them automatically with exponential backoff.
docsRetries.problemDesc1: Webhook deliveries fail all the time. The receiver's server might be down, the network might be slow, or a deployment might be in progress. If you only try once, you lose the event.
docsRetries.problemDesc2: But retrying blindly is also bad — if you retry immediately and aggressively, you overwhelm a server that's already struggling.
docsRetries.howHandlesDesc: HookSniff uses exponential backoff with jitter:
docsRetries.exponentialBackoff: Exponential backoff — Each retry waits longer than the last (1s → 2s → 4s)
docsRetries.jitter: Jitter (0-25%) — Random variation prevents thundering herd when many deliveries fail simultaneously
docsRetries.configurable: Configurable — Customize per endpoint: max attempts, base delay, max delay, multiplier
docsRetries.platformDefault: Platform default: 3 attempts. Configurable per endpoint from 1 to 100.
docsRetries.whatCountsDesc: Not all errors trigger retries. HookSniff distinguishes between retryable and non-retryable failures:
docsRetries.only2xx: Only 2xx responses are considered successful. Client errors (4xx except 429/408) indicate a problem with the request itself — retrying won't help.
docsRetries.customPolicyDesc: Different endpoints have different needs. A critical payment webhook might need more retries than a low-priority notification. Configure per endpoint:
docsRetries.replayingDesc: After a delivery is moved to the DLQ, you can replay it. This resets the attempt counter and re-queues with a fresh retry schedule:
docsRetries.replayingTip: You can also replay from the dashboard with one click. Use this after fixing the issue on the receiver's side.
docsRetries.paymentWebhooks: Payment webhooks — Increase max_attempts to 5-10, payments are critical
docsRetries.analyticsEvents: Analytics events — Decrease to 1-2, not worth retrying aggressively
docsRetries.slowReceivers: Slow receivers — Increase base_delay_ms to give them more recovery time
docsRetries.highVolume: High-volume endpoints — Use higher multiplier to space out retries

docsRateLimiting.title: Rate Limiting
docsRateLimiting.subtitle: HookSniff rate limits API requests per account to protect the platform and ensure fair usage.
docsRateLimiting.howItWorksDesc: HookSniff uses a sliding window algorithm. Each API key has a requests-per-minute limit based on your plan. When you exceed the limit, requests return 429 Too Many Requests.
docsRateLimiting.howItWorksNote: Rate limits apply to all authenticated API endpoints. Health checks and public endpoints are not rate limited.
docsRateLimiting.yearlyBilling: Yearly billing: 20% discount. Overage pricing: Startup $0.003/event, Pro $0.0001/event.
docsRateLimiting.rateLimitHeadersDesc: Every API response includes rate limit headers:
docsRateLimiting.whenRateLimited: When rate limited (429), the response includes:
docsRateLimiting.handlingStep2: Wait for the number of seconds in the Retry-After header
docsRateLimiting.handlingStep3: Resume with exponential backoff if retries continue
docsRateLimiting.monthlyLimitsDesc: Each plan has a monthly webhook delivery limit. This counts successful API calls to POST /v1/webhooks, not deliveries to endpoints.
docsRateLimiting.checkUsage: Check your current usage via the API:
docsRateLimiting.perEndpointDesc: In addition to account-level rate limits, HookSniff supports per-endpoint throttling to protect your customers' servers. This uses a token bucket or sliding window algorithm configured per endpoint.
docsRateLimiting.perEndpointWhen1: A customer's server can only handle a certain number of webhooks per second
docsRateLimiting.perEndpointWhen2: You want to prevent burst traffic from overwhelming a slow endpoint
docsRateLimiting.perEndpointWhen3: You need different rate limits for different customers

docsErrorHandling.title: Client Error Handling
docsErrorHandling.subtitle: How to handle webhook errors gracefully in your receiving endpoint.
docsErrorHandling.problemDesc: Your webhook endpoint will receive all kinds of events — some expected, some not. Some payloads will be valid, some might be malformed. Your endpoint needs to handle all of these gracefully without crashing.
docsErrorHandling.respondFastDesc: Always return 200 within 5 seconds. Do the actual processing asynchronously:
docsErrorHandling.unknownEventsDesc: Your endpoint might receive event types you don't expect. Don't crash — acknowledge and skip:
docsErrorHandling.duplicatesDesc: Webhooks can be delivered more than once. Always check the delivery ID:
docsErrorHandling.statusCodesTip: Only return 5xx if you want HookSniff to retry. Return 4xx if the problem is on your side and retrying won't help.
docsErrorHandling.gracefulDegradationDesc: If your processing fails, still return 200 and log the error. You can replay the webhook later:

docsDlq.title: Dead Letter Queue (DLQ)
docsDlq.subtitle: Failed deliveries shouldn't vanish. The DLQ preserves them so you can understand what went wrong and fix it.
docsDlq.problemDesc1: After all retries are exhausted, what happens to the event? In most webhook systems, it's silently dropped. You never know it failed, and the data is lost forever.
docsDlq.problemDesc2: This is especially painful for critical events like payments or order updates — losing them means lost revenue or unhappy customers.
docsDlq.howWorksDesc: When a delivery exhausts all retry attempts, HookSniff moves it to the Dead Letter Queue instead of dropping it. The DLQ preserves everything:
docsDlq.originalPayload: Original payload — The full event data
docsDlq.allAttempts: All attempt details — Status codes, response bodies, timestamps for every retry
docsDlq.timing: Timing — When each attempt was made and how long it took
docsDlq.errorContext: Error context — Why each attempt failed
docsDlq.inspectTip: You can inspect DLQ entries via the API or dashboard, and replay them with one click after fixing the issue.
docsDlq.whenDlq: When Do Webhooks Go to the DLQ?
docsDlq.whenDlqDesc: A delivery is moved to the DLQ when:
docsDlq.whenDlq1: All retry attempts have been exhausted (default: 3 attempts, configurable per endpoint)
docsDlq.whenDlq3: The delivery has been pending for too long (stale delivery timeout)
docsDlq.failure429: Endpoint returning 429 rate limits that don't clear
docsDlq.inspectingDesc: Query failed deliveries via the API:
docsDlq.replayingDesc: Once you've fixed the issue, replay the delivery. This resets the attempt counter and re-queues with a fresh retry schedule:
docsDlq.replayingTip: You can also replay from the dashboard with one click. Batch replay is available for multiple failed deliveries.
docsDlq.retentionDesc: DLQ entries are retained based on your plan:
docsDlq.retentionExpired: After retention expires, DLQ entries are permanently deleted. Export important data before it expires.

docsEventTypes.title: Event Types
docsEventTypes.subtitle: Event types let you categorize webhooks and route them to the right endpoints. Without them, every webhook goes everywhere.
docsEventTypes.problemDesc1: If your application sends different kinds of events — order updates, user signups, payment confirmations — you probably want different endpoints to handle them. Your order processing service shouldn't receive user signup events.
docsEventTypes.problemDesc2: Without event types, you'd need to filter on the consumer side, wasting bandwidth and processing time.
docsEventTypes.howWorksDesc: Event types are string identifiers that describe what happened. They follow a resource.action pattern:
docsEventTypes.anyFormat: You can use any string format, but resource.action is recommended for consistency.
docsEventTypes.registeringDesc: Event types are automatically registered when you send a webhook. No pre-registration required:
docsEventTypes.nowAvailable: The event type order.created is now available for filtering and search.
docsEventTypes.filteringDesc: Configure endpoints to only receive specific event types using the event_filter field:
docsEventTypes.schemaValidationDesc: Optionally define JSON schemas for event types to validate payloads before delivery:
docsEventTypes.schemaReject: When a schema is defined, payloads that don't match are rejected with a 400 Bad Request error before they even enter the delivery pipeline.
docsEventTypes.bpSpecific: Be specific: invoice.payment_failed is better than invoice.error
docsEventTypes.bpDots: Use dots as separators: resource.action format
docsEventTypes.bpWildcards: Use wildcards for grouping: payment.* catches all payment events
docsEventTypes.bpDocument: Document your event types: Keep a list of all event types your application emits

docsEmbedPortal.title: Embeddable Portal
docsEmbedPortal.subtitle: Let your customers manage their own webhooks — without building a UI from scratch.
docsEmbedPortal.problemDesc: Every SaaS that offers webhooks needs a UI for customers to manage endpoints, view logs, and replay events. Building this from scratch takes weeks.
docsEmbedPortal.howEmbedDesc: The portal is a React component you embed in your app. It connects to HookSniff via API and gives your customers a full webhook management UI.
docsEmbedPortal.usageDesc: Embed the portal in your app:
docsEmbedPortal.customizationDesc: Customize the portal's appearance to match your brand:
docsEmbedPortal.feature1: Endpoint management — create, edit, delete endpoints
docsEmbedPortal.feature2: Delivery logs — view all deliveries with status, timing, and payload
docsEmbedPortal.feature3: Replay — one-click replay of failed deliveries
docsEmbedPortal.feature4: Secret rotation — rotate signing secrets without downtime
docsEmbedPortal.feature5: Event filtering — configure which events each endpoint receives
docsEmbedPortal.feature6: Theme support — light/dark mode, custom colors

docsWebhookVsPolling.title: Webhook vs Polling
docsWebhookVsPolling.subtitle: Two approaches to getting real-time data. Each has trade-offs.
docsWebhookVsPolling.problemDesc: Your application needs to know when something happens in another system. You have two options: poll for changes or receive webhooks.
docsWebhookVsPolling.pollingDesc: Polling means asking the server 'anything new?' at regular intervals. Simple to implement, but wasteful.
docsWebhookVsPolling.pollingCon1: Wastes bandwidth — most polls return 'nothing new'
docsWebhookVsPolling.pollingCon2: Delayed detection — you only find out on your next poll
docsWebhookVsPolling.pollingCon3: Rate limiting — APIs limit how often you can poll
docsWebhookVsPolling.pollingCon4: Scales poorly — 1000 customers polling every 5s = 200 requests/sec
docsWebhookVsPolling.webhooksDesc: Webhooks mean the server sends you a notification when something happens. More efficient, but requires setup.
docsWebhookVsPolling.webhookPro1: Real-time — notified instantly when something happens
docsWebhookVsPolling.webhookPro3: Scales well — server handles the load
docsWebhookVsPolling.webhookCon2: Need to handle retries and failures
docsWebhookVsPolling.whenWebhooks3: You want to reduce API calls
docsWebhookVsPolling.hybridDesc: Many systems use both: webhooks for real-time updates, polling as a fallback. HookSniff supports this pattern with its message poller API.

docsDebugFailed.title: Debug Failed Webhooks
docsDebugFailed.subtitle: A webhook failed. Now what? Here's how to find out why and fix it.
docsDebugFailed.step1Desc: Check delivery status via the API or dashboard:
docsDebugFailed.step2Field2: Response body (the error message from the endpoint)
docsDebugFailed.step2Field4: Error context (DNS failure, TLS error, etc.)
docsDebugFailed.step3Desc: Based on the error, fix the appropriate part:
docsDebugFailed.fix5xx: 5xx errors — Fix your server. Check logs, database connections, dependencies.
docsDebugFailed.fix401: 401 Unauthorized — Check if your endpoint expects authentication. Add the required headers.
docsDebugFailed.fix404: 404 Not Found — Your endpoint URL is wrong or the route doesn't exist.
docsDebugFailed.fixTimeout: Timeout — Your endpoint takes too long. Optimize processing or use async pattern.
docsDebugFailed.fixDns: DNS failure — Your domain doesn't resolve. Check DNS records.
docsDebugFailed.fixTls: TLS error — Certificate issue. Renew or fix your SSL certificate.
docsDebugFailed.step4Desc: After fixing the issue, replay the failed delivery:
docsDebugFailed.step4Tip: You can also replay from the dashboard with one click.
docsDebugFailed.patternIntermittent: Intermittent 5xx — Usually a deployment or scaling issue. Check if failures correlate with deploy times.
docsDebugFailed.patternAllFailed: All deliveries failing — Your endpoint is down. Check server health.
docsDebugFailed.patternSlowDegradation: Slow degradation — Success rate drops over time. Usually a memory leak or connection pool exhaustion.
docsDebugFailed.patternBurst: Burst failures — Many failures at once. Usually a rate limit or downstream dependency issue.

docsEventProcessing.title: Event Processing
docsEventProcessing.subtitle: Understanding the event lifecycle helps you debug issues and optimize your integration.
docsEventProcessing.lifecycleDesc: Every event goes through these stages:
docsEventProcessing.stage2: Validation — Payload checked, rate limits applied
docsEventProcessing.stage4: Queuing — Delivery queued for each endpoint
docsEventProcessing.stage5: Delivery — HTTP POST sent to endpoint
docsEventProcessing.stage6: Verification — Response recorded, retry scheduled if needed
docsEventProcessing.stage7: Completion — Delivery marked as delivered or failed
docsEventProcessing.ingestionDesc: When you send an event via POST /v1/webhooks, HookSniff:
docsEventProcessing.ingestionStep2: Checks idempotency key (returns cached response if duplicate)
docsEventProcessing.ingestionStep3: Validates event type format and payload size
docsEventProcessing.ingestionStep5: Finds matching endpoints (active, matching event filter)
docsEventProcessing.ingestionStep6: Creates delivery records and queues them
docsEventProcessing.routingDesc: HookSniff finds all active endpoints that match the event type:
docsEventProcessing.routingExact: Exact match: order.created matches only order.created
docsEventProcessing.routingWildcard: Wildcard: order.* matches order.created, order.updated, etc.
docsEventProcessing.deliveryDesc: The worker picks up queued deliveries and sends HTTP POST requests:
docsEventProcessing.deliveryStep4: Records the response (status code, body, timing)
docsEventProcessing.deliveryStep6: On failure: schedules retry or moves to DLQ

docsMultiTenant.title: Multi-Tenant Architecture
docsMultiTenant.subtitle: Building a SaaS platform? Use HookSniff to let each customer manage their own webhooks.
docsMultiTenant.problemDesc: In a multi-tenant SaaS, each customer needs isolated webhook infrastructure. You don't want Customer A seeing Customer B's events.
docsMultiTenant.howWorksDesc: HookSniff supports multi-tenancy through API key isolation. Each customer gets their own API key, and all resources are scoped to that key.
docsMultiTenant.implementationDesc: Create a separate API key for each customer:
docsMultiTenant.isolationDesc: Each API key provides complete isolation:
docsMultiTenant.isolation1: Endpoints — each customer sees only their own
docsMultiTenant.isolation3: API keys — independent rate limits per key
docsMultiTenant.bp1: Use descriptive key names: customer_acme_prod, customer_acme_staging
docsMultiTenant.bp3: Use webhook event filtering to route events to the right customer
docsMultiTenant.bp4: Monitor per-customer usage for billing and abuse detection
docsMultiTenant.bp5: Implement proper error handling per customer
